name: Turpin
description: Archbishop Turpin is a legendary figure from the Matter of France, particularly the chansons de geste. He is depicted as a valiant warrior-priest, a loyal companion to Charlemagne, and a key figure in the battles against the Saracens. Turpin's origins are shrouded in legend, but he is consistently portrayed as the Archbishop of Reims, a powerful and influential figure in the Carolingian court. He is not merely a religious leader; he actively participates in battle, wielding weapons and inspiring the Frankish troops with his faith and courage. Turpin's presence elevates the conflict to a holy war, imbuing the Frankish cause with divine purpose. He is often depicted as performing miraculous feats and offering comfort to the wounded and dying. His death in battle alongside Roland and the other paladins at Roncevaux Pass is a pivotal moment in the Song of Roland, solidifying his status as a martyr for Christendom. Turpin represents the ideal of the warrior-priest, embodying both spiritual authority and martial prowess. He is a symbol of faith, courage, and loyalty, and his legend has inspired generations. He is a reminder of the close relationship between religion and warfare in medieval Europe, and his story continues to resonate with audiences today. Turpin's character is a blend of historical and fictional elements, making him a compelling and enduring figure in French literature and folklore. He is the archetype of a fighting bishop, a figure willing to defend his faith with both prayer and sword. His role is crucial in shaping the narrative of the Carolingian era and its conflicts, cementing his place as a legendary hero.
subtitle: Warrior-Priest of the Carolingian Empire
achievements: Dying a martyr at Roncevaux Pass

keyMyths.title: Battle of Roncevaux Pass
keyMyths.description: Turpin fights valiantly alongside Roland and the other paladins against the Saracen forces at Roncevaux Pass. He dies a heroic death, solidifying his status as a martyr.

keyMyths.title: Turpin's Blessing
keyMyths.description: Before battles, Turpin would bless the Frankish soldiers, imbuing them with courage and faith. His blessings were believed to offer protection and divine favor in combat.

keyMyths.title: Miracles on the Battlefield
keyMyths.description: Accounts claim Turpin performed miracles during battle, such as healing the wounded or summoning divine aid. These acts reinforced his image as a divinely favored warrior.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: The most famous account of Turpin's life and death.
sources.text: The most famous account of Turpin's life and death.

sources.author: Pseudo-Turpin
sources.description: A Latin prose history attributed to Turpin, but actually written later. Presents a highly embellished account of Charlemagne's campaigns.
sources.text: A Latin prose history attributed to Turpin, but actually written later. Presents a highly embellished account of Charlemagne's campaigns.
